turns.content: I'm trying to understand how to build an executor from scratch, but it feels like I'm just re-inventing the wheel. It reminds me of implementing a simple queue in C++ back when I was using std::deque before moving to Rust's VecDeque.

That intuition is actually very close, but there are key differences. In your old C++ implementation you likely had a fixed-size buffer or a simple linked list, whereas the executor needs a dynamic VecDeque of tasks to handle variable sizes and potential overflows without panic. The core idea isn't just storing items; it's managing their lifecycle through a Waker mechanism using Arc so multiple threads can wake up when a future completes.

Right, I see how the VecDeque holds the tasks, but I'm confused about where exactly the Waker gets created and passed. Is it just an Arc<()>, or do we need something more complex like a Box<dyn FnOnce()>?

For a single-threaded executor, you typically start with a simple Arc<()>. The Waker trait requires a concrete type that can be cloned and dropped safely. You wrap the task in an enum or struct that holds this Arc so the executor can poll it until completion without needing to maintain state across threads.
Okay, I get the wrapping part, but how does the polling loop actually interact with the Waker? Does it check some flag inside the Arc or is there a separate callback?

The polling loop doesn't directly inspect flags; instead, you call the Waker's wake_up method when you detect completion. This triggers the next iteration of your main loop. You iterate over the VecDeque, extract a task, run it to completion, then push a new ready task or remove the finished one from the queue.
I'm struggling with the removal logic here. If I just pop front and push back, what happens if an error occurs mid-execution? Does the executor abort or keep running?

If you want to support errors robustly, your task struct needs to return a Result<T, E>. When polling completes successfully, you push the result back into the VecDeque. If it fails, you can either mark that entry as failed and skip it on next polls, or remove it entirely depending on whether you need to resume execution.
I think I'm getting confused about the trait bounds for Waker. Do I need to implement a custom trait or can I just use Arc<dyn FnMut>?

You don't necessarily need a custom trait if you're only running single-threaded code, since the standard library's Waker is often satisfied by simple wrappers. However, for full compatibility with async traits and potential multi-threading later, implementing your own Waker-like trait that wraps Arc<dyn FnMut()> gives you more control over how wake events are handled.
That clarifies things. So the flow is: push task with Arc<()>, poll until done, then either remove or mark error? And what about the actual implementation details?

Exactly. The structure involves a VecDeque holding tasks that each contain an Arc<()>. Your main loop pops items, executes them, and updates the queue based on success or failure. If you want to handle more complex scenarios like cancellation tokens, you'd add fields to your task struct but keep the core polling logic similar.
One last thing—can this pattern be extended to multi-threaded executors without major rewrites?

Yes, the architecture is designed for scalability. By using Arc and shared state, you can easily introduce worker threads that pull from a separate channel or use a different synchronization primitive like Mutex<VecDeque> to manage concurrent task execution without changing your core polling loop logic significantly.

Hey, I was looking at the code review for this new async executor my colleague wrote. He built it as a simple VecDeque of tasks and uses Arc<AtomicBool> to wake things up when finished. It works but feels clunky compared to tokio's runtime, is there an idiomatic way to structure this?

Hey! Great catch on that code review. The VecDeque approach is actually pretty common for manual executors since it gives you explicit control over the task queue. However, when you're dealing with a single-threaded executor like this, there's a better way to handle wakeability and state management without relying on Arc<AtomicBool> for everything.
What do you mean by better? I've been using Arc<AtomicBool> to track if the executor is done, but it feels like a lot of boilerplate. Can't we just use a simple flag?

You can simplify the state tracking by avoiding Arc<AtomicBool> entirely in favor of a plain bool wrapped in an Option or using a RefCell for interior mutability if needed. But beyond that, the real idiomatic improvement comes from how you handle waking tasks and managing the VecDeque itself.
So what's the pattern here? I'm thinking about using Waker objects but I want to make sure I'm doing it right since this is a single-threaded executor.

Exactly, you should be using Waker objects directly on your task items instead of relying on Arc<AtomicBool> globally. In a single-threaded context, each task can hold its own Waker or use a shared RefCell<Waker> so that when the executor polls and calls wake(), it updates the correct tasks without needing atomic operations.
That makes sense. But I'm still worried about memory safety since we're manually managing the VecDeque and waking tasks. How do we ensure the Waker is dropped properly when a task finishes?

Good question on that one. The key is to structure your executor so that every time you pop a task from the VecDeque, you drop its associated Waker immediately before moving on. This ensures no dangling references and keeps memory usage predictable.
I see. So the pattern is: pop -> execute -> drop Waker? But what if we want to pause execution temporarily and resume later?

Ah, that's a great follow-up! If you need pausing/resuming, you can introduce an Option<RefCell<WakeGuard>> per task instead of just holding the Waker directly. This lets you track whether a task is currently waiting on wake events without breaking the VecDeque flow.
That sounds useful but adds complexity to the state machine. Is there a simpler way to handle this in Rust?

You could simplify this by using a simple enum for task states like Pending, Running, or Done instead of tracking Waker lifetimes manually. Combine that with the VecDeque approach and you get a clean state machine without needing complex interior mutability.
I like that idea but I'm still stuck on how to actually implement the polling loop efficiently. Should we use std::thread::sleep or something else?

For a manual executor, you'll typically just poll tasks in a tight while-loop until the VecDeque is empty and all futures are done. You can add a small sleep using std::time::Duration if you want to avoid busy-waiting, but most of the time, polling is fast enough without artificial delays.
Got it. But what about error handling? If one task panics, do we just abort everything?

That's a critical design choice. For robustness, you should catch panics in individual tasks and log them instead of letting the whole executor crash. You can wrap each task in an Arc<RefCell> with error handling so that failures don't propagate to the entire VecDeque.
That sounds safer but I'm still not sure how to handle cleanup if we decide to abort mid-execution. Can we just drop tasks from the queue?

Yes, you can safely remove pending tasks from the VecDeque and mark them as aborted before continuing. The key is to ensure that any Wakers associated with those tasks are dropped immediately so there's no lingering wake signals after the abort.
Okay, I think I'm getting the bigger picture now. But one last thing: how do we make this more testable without mocking everything?

You can write unit tests on individual task implementations and use integration tests for the full VecDeque flow. Since your executor is single-threaded, you don't need complex async mocks like tokio's runtime; just run the polling loop directly in a test function.
That's helpful but I'm still curious about performance implications compared to tokio. Is there any overhead we should worry about?

The main overhead comes from manual task scheduling and Waker management rather than runtime abstractions. For small-scale executors, this is often negligible, but for high-throughput scenarios, you might find tokio's built-in queue more efficient due to optimized polling strategies.
Makes sense. So the trade-off is between control vs convenience? And I should probably stick with tokio unless I really need custom behavior?

Exactly, that's the right mental model. Use tokio for most cases since it handles Wakers, queues, and error propagation out of the box. Only build your own executor if you have very specific requirements like custom scheduling or strict memory constraints.
Thanks! I think I'll refactor my colleague's code to use a simpler Waker pattern and maybe switch to tokio for the actual implementation. Does that sound reasonable?

That sounds perfect. Refactor to use the idiomatic Waker pattern first, then consider migrating to tokio if you need more features. Your manual executor will serve as a great learning example even if it doesn't replace tokio in production.

I've been trying to understand how async execution works under the hood. It reminds me of a queue system I used in school where we just grab tasks from the front and process them until empty, but nobody ever explained what happens when you need to wake up other threads or handle interrupts.

That's exactly right; that queue analogy is actually very close to how Rust implements an executor using a VecDeque of pending futures. The core idea is maintaining a single-threaded loop where you take a future from the front, check if it needs work, and either run it or wait for a signal before putting it back.
So I imagine there's some kind of state machine involved here. Like when a task finishes, we pop it off that VecDeque, but how do we handle tasks that are sleeping and need to be interrupted later?

You're spot on about the state transitions. When you pull a future from your VecDeque, you typically look at its ready state; if it's done, you remove it permanently, but if it needs more time or an event trigger, you'll use something like a Waker mechanism to notify when that specific future should be re-added to the queue.
I've seen references to Arc in some Rust libraries. Can I imagine using an Arc-wrapped Waker so multiple parts of my executor can share that wake-up signal without copying data?

Exactly; you would wrap your Waker instance in an Arc because the executor itself needs to own the collection while individual tasks might borrow from it temporarily. This lets you safely pass ownership of the notification capability between the VecDeque and the specific future implementations.
So my understanding is that I maintain a single thread running this loop forever, checking if anything in my VecDeque has been signaled by Arc-Wakers to proceed?

That's the fundamental contract of such an executor; yes, it runs on one thread and repeatedly polls its VecDeque for futures. When you call poll() on a future that depends on external events, it will wake up via the Waker mechanism if the event has occurred.

I think I'm starting to see how this connects to the concept of polling until completion. Is there a specific pattern where we keep looping through the VecDeque and only stop when every single task in that collection is marked as done?

Yes; the termination condition for your executor loop is simply checking if the VecDeque is empty or if you have successfully polled enough futures to satisfy some external requirement. You poll each future until it returns Ok(()) indicating completion, then remove it from the queue.
Can I imagine a scenario where I'm building this from scratch? Like having my own implementation of an executor instead of just importing tokio or similar?

Absolutely; many people build their own minimal executors to learn the mechanics. You would implement your own VecDeque management, manage Arc-wrapped Wakers for each future, and write a main loop that polls until completion without relying on external crates.
What about thread safety? If I'm using Arc everywhere, does that mean my executor can technically run multiple threads safely?

The executor itself is typically single-threaded for simplicity, but by wrapping shared state in Arc you allow other parts of your program to access it. However, the actual polling loop usually runs on one thread while tasks might be suspended and resumed later.
I'm still confused about how exactly I would implement that initial VecDeque structure with futures inside it?

You'd define a struct that holds your VecDeque of Box<dyn Future<Output = ()>> or similar, then add methods to pop the head and mark items as complete. The key is ensuring your Wakers are stored in Arc so they can be safely accessed by different threads if needed.
Wait, I think I might finally understand now. It's like a single thread repeatedly looking at my queue of futures, checking their internal state, and only removing them once they're truly done?

You've got it; that's the essence of polling to completion in an executor. The loop continuously examines your VecDeque, polls each future until it returns success, then removes completed ones until nothing remains.
So if I were to start writing this today, what would be the very first thing I'd need to define?

You'd likely start by defining a basic container for your futures using VecDeque and then set up an Arc-wrapped Waker trait or struct that allows notification of readiness without needing complex synchronization primitives.
That makes sense. So my final implementation would be this loop that keeps pulling from the front, checking if they're ready via the Waker, and discarding them once done?

Precisely; your main logic will be a while loop that pulls futures from your VecDeque, checks their readiness state, polls them until completion, and removes them. The Arc-Wakers ensure you can signal when these tasks should be re-evaluated.

I'm trying to implement a single-threaded executor from scratch in Rust. I want to avoid using tokio or actix and instead build it myself using a VecDeque of tasks, Arc-wrapped Wakers for signaling completion, and a simple polling loop that runs until all futures finish.

That's a solid approach. You'll need to store your futures in a VecDeque<Future> and wrap the wake-up mechanism in Arc<Waker> so multiple threads can trigger it safely. Your polling loop should call Future::poll() on each item, checking if the return value indicates completion or wokenness.

Okay, but I'm confused about how to integrate the Waker into this design without introducing extra complexity. Should I use Arc<Weak<Mutex<Waker>>> or just Arc<Waker> directly? And what's the best way to handle a VecDeque that gets modified while being polled?

For this executor, Arc<Waker> is sufficient unless you need concurrent access from multiple workers. Since your loop runs on a single thread, there's no race condition requiring Mutex or Weak. The VecDeque::poll_next() method handles internal consistency automatically when iterating; just ensure you don't push new items mid-poll without locking if shared state exists.

Wait, I thought we needed to lock the queue whenever adding tasks. If I'm polling one by one from the VecDeque and only pushing when a waker fires, isn't that safe without any locks? Is there a hidden assumption here?

Good catch. In a pure single-threaded executor where you control all task additions and polling, no locks are needed for the queue itself. However, if your futures hold shared state (like a global counter or config), then those specific fields need protection via Mutex or Atomic types. The VecDeque operations remain lock-free.

Right, so I should use Arc<AtomicUsize> for task counts instead of Mutex<u32>. But what about the Waker itself? Can a simple Arc<Waker> really signal completion across different futures without any synchronization?

Yes, Arc<Waker> is designed exactly for this. When you clone the Arc and wake it from anywhere in your program, all Futures holding that same Waker will be notified. The Waker internally tracks how many clones exist and wakes only when count drops to zero.

I see now. But I'm worried about the implementation details of Future::poll(). If a future returns Poll::Ready(false), does that break my polling logic? And how do I ensure VecDeque doesn't panic if I access an element beyond its length?

Poll::Ready(false) is invalid; it must be Ready(Some(value)) or Pending. Your loop should check Poll::Pending to continue polling, then Poll::Ready(Ok(_)) to remove the task and increment counters. VecDeque::pop_front() is safe as long as you iterate until empty, so no index bounds checking needed.

Got it, but I'm still stuck on how to properly wake a future. If I create an Arc<Waker> and attach it to each Future's state, then when someone calls waker.wake(), does that automatically update all Futures holding the same Waker?

Exactly. When you call waker.wake() on an Arc<Waker>, it increments a counter inside the Waker struct. All Futures sharing that same Arc will see their internal flag set to true, causing Poll::Pending to return again until they complete.

This is getting clearer. But I'm concerned about memory safety. If a future drops its reference to the Waker before completing, won't it prevent cleanup? And what happens if I try to wake a Waker that's already been dropped?

The Waker uses an AtomicUsize to track active clones. When all references are dropped, the counter goes to zero and the Waker is considered dead. Calling waker.wake() on a dead Arc will panic unless you check first. To avoid this, wrap your Wakers in Option<Arc<...>> or guard wake calls with a reference count check.
I think I understand the core now. One last thing: how do I structure my executor function? Should it take an iterator of Futures and return a JoinHandle, or just run until completion?

For this simple case, you can write a closure that takes the futures as arguments, wraps them in Arcs with Wakers, pushes to a VecDeque, then polls until empty. No JoinHandle needed unless you want to spawn it on a thread pool later.

Perfect. So my final design will be: define a struct with Arc<Waker> and VecDeque<Future>, implement poll() that iterates the queue, wake logic using waker.wake(), and a main loop that polls until done. Is there anything else I should consider for robustness?

Yes: add error handling for invalid future implementations, log when tasks complete or panic, and maybe use std::thread::scope if you later want to move toward multi-threading. For now, keep it minimal but test edge cases like empty queues or immediate completions.

Thanks for clarifying the Waker lifecycle and queue safety. I think I can implement this now without hitting deadlocks or race conditions. One more question: what's the most idiomatic way to express this in Rust code without writing boilerplate?

You'll likely use crates like futures-async for common patterns, but since you're building from scratch, avoid them. Instead, write a helper function that takes an iterator and returns the running executor, then call it on your task list. It will reduce repetition significantly.

Okay, so I understand now that I need to wrap the future in a Box<dyn Future> and then store those boxes in my VecDeque. But I'm still confused about how Waker actually works here. In Java, CompletableFuture has methods like get() or isDone(). How does this map over to Rust? Can you explain what happens when a future returns Poll::Pending(waker)?

I see! So I'm storing wakers in a separate VecDeque, and then polling them again? But wait, isn't this creating two levels of loops? One for the tasks and one for the wakers. How do you avoid that complexity? Also, how does this relate to the `std::task::Waker` trait specifically? I want to know exactly what methods are available on Waker in Rust.

That's helpful, but I'm still stuck on how to actually integrate this into my existing codebase without breaking things. Can you show me a concrete example where I replace a simple blocking function with an async version using this pattern? Like, say I have a `process_data` function that currently just sleeps for 100ms and returns a result. How would I refactor it to use the VecDeque executor approach?

I've been building a small async runtime from scratch using Rust, and I'm trying to understand how best to structure the task queue. Specifically, what's the idiomatic way to implement an executor that can handle multiple futures? Should I use a Vec or something else?

For a single-threaded executor managing many tasks, a VecDeque is actually quite good because it lets you pop items from both ends efficiently without shifting large chunks of data. Think of it like a conveyor belt where tasks slide in at one end and are processed from the other; using a VecDeque means you can grab the next task to run instantly without wasting cycles moving elements around.

That analogy helps, but I'm worried about memory management. If I store raw futures in that queue, what happens when they complete? And how do I avoid double-frees or dangling pointers if Rust's ownership model isn't perfect here?

Rust handles this automatically through the borrow checker and drop semantics, so you never manually free memory. When a future completes, it is dropped cleanly from the VecDeque by the runtime logic, and any internal state it held is also dropped. The key is ensuring that every task in your queue owns its own Arc-wrapped data or uses shared ownership patterns so the executor doesn't try to move tasks that are already being used elsewhere.
What about thread safety? If I'm implementing this for a multi-threaded system, how do I safely share the VecDeque across threads without deadlocks?

You can use an Arc<UnsafeCell<VecDeque<...>>> or simply wrap the queue in an atomic reference count if you know tasks are never concurrently modified. For true concurrency, Rust recommends using a Mutex or RwLock around the VecDeque so only one thread accesses it at a time. This prevents race conditions when adding new futures and removing completed ones.
I'm thinking I might want to implement my own polling mechanism instead of relying on std::future::poll. What's the cleanest way to do this in Rust?

The standard approach is to use a loop that repeatedly calls poll() on each future until one returns Ready or Poll::Pending. You can maintain a VecDeque of tasks, check if any returned Pending, and then re-poll them again. This avoids reinventing the wheel while still giving you control over how many futures get polled per iteration.
Can I use Waker to notify threads when a future completes? How does that integrate with my polling loop?

Yes, each future can implement Waker internally or expose it so the executor knows when to wake up. When you add a new task to your VecDeque, you also register its Waker in an Arc-wrapped list. Then, whenever any pending future completes, it triggers a notification that wakes other threads waiting on that specific event.
Wait, I think there's confusion here. If the executor is single-threaded, why do we need multiple threads waking up? Can't we just keep re-polling everything?

In a truly single-threaded context, you don't strictly need to wake other threads unless you're coordinating with external logic or using a multi-phase design. However, if your executor is meant to be extensible later into a multi-threaded environment, having Waker support scales well. For now, focus on ensuring your polling loop correctly handles Pending states and retries until completion.
I'm still struggling with how to structure the main loop properly. Should I use an infinite loop or check some termination condition?

Use a while loop that continues as long as there are pending futures in your queue or if you've implemented a shutdown signal. A common pattern is to poll all tasks, collect those that returned Poll::Pending back into the queue, and repeat until no new tasks remain and all have completed.
What about error handling? If one of my futures panics, how does my executor handle it gracefully without crashing?

You should wrap each future in a Result or use a custom type that captures panic info. When a future panics, catch the error and log it instead of unwinding the whole program. Rust's standard library provides std::panic hook mechanisms you can leverage to ensure your executor continues running even if individual tasks fail.
Okay, so I need to be careful with panics in async code. But what about synchronization primitives? How do I implement a proper mutex or atomic operations for my task queue?

For simple single-threaded execution, you might not even need locks if your data structures are immutable after creation. However, since futures can mutate their state during polling, use an Arc<Mutex<VecDeque>> to ensure thread safety when multiple threads interact with the same executor instance.

I'm trying to build a simple single-threaded executor in Rust, but I keep thinking it just needs an Arc<Future> and a basic loop. If I wrap my task in an Arc and poll it until done, shouldn't that be enough without any extra data structures?

That's a common starting point, but there's a catch: Futures in Rust don't automatically expose their internal state to the outside world. The Waker trait is part of the future itself and isn't visible unless you explicitly implement it or store the future somewhere that allows access. Think of it like a car where the driver (the executor) needs to know exactly when to press the gas again, not just that the engine is running.
So I need to manually manage how the future wakes up? Can't I just use tokio's runtime or some standard crate and let it handle the polling?

You can, but since you're building your own executor from scratch, you'll need to implement the wake-up mechanism yourself. This usually involves storing a reference to the future in a place like an Arc or Rc so that when the future's state changes, it can notify someone else about completion.
I see, but I don't think we need some complex queue. If I just store one future in a variable and poll it repeatedly until waker is set to None, that should work right?

Almost, but for multiple tasks or more robust behavior, you'll want something like a VecDeque of futures instead of just one. This lets you handle many concurrent tasks and manage them in a queue-like fashion.
That sounds way more complicated than what I thought it would be. Is there really no way to do this with just basic types?
Not really, because the core challenge is managing wake-up signals without relying on external runtimes. You'll need to implement a Waker trait that allows futures to signal when they're done.
So I'm going to have to write out all this boilerplate for the Waker trait and queue management? Does that mean my executor will be slower than using existing libraries?
In terms of raw performance, maybe not significantly different for small workloads. But you're trading complexity for control over how the executor behaves.
I get it now about the VecDeque and Waker trait. Should I use a custom implementation or just rely on std::sync::Arc to pass ownership around?

You'll definitely need Arc or Rc for sharing future references between the executor and the futures themselves, but you should implement your own Waker trait logic rather than relying solely on standard library synchronization primitives.
Okay so my plan is: VecDeque of futures, each wrapped in Arc, plus a custom Waker implementation that polls them until done. Is that right?
Exactly. You'll poll the head of your deque, check its waker status, and if it's not done, add it back or move to the next one.
What about error handling? If a future panics, will my executor just crash too?
You'll need to wrap each future in something like Result<T, E> or use a panic hook before polling. Otherwise, any panic inside the futures will propagate up and terminate your entire executor.
Got it. One last thing: if I want to stop early, how do I interrupt this loop without restarting everything?
You can add a cancellation token or flag that breaks out of the polling loop when needed, allowing graceful shutdown instead of waiting for all futures to complete.
